segments.s733-g1: ازان پس (literally 'from that after') is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that'; چو introduces a circumstantial clause 'when'. گسترده شد is a past passive/perfect verbal phrase 'was/went on being spread' or 'spread (wide)' with شد as the past auxiliary/verb. دست شاه places the possessor after the noun: 'the king's hand/power' (دست here means 'power/control'), so the clause reads 'after that, when the king's power spread'.
segments.s733-g2: سراسر جهان شد is a predicative clause: سراسر (an adjective/adverb) qualifies جهان ('the whole/world') and شد is the past of شدن 'became'. ورا (vora) is a short form of 'به او' meaning 'to him', and نیک‌خواه functions as a noun/adjective 'well-wisher/benevolent (one)'. Together: 'the whole world became (a) well-wisher to him.'
segments.s733-g3: همه before a noun means 'all/everyone'. تاجدارانش is the plural noun 'crown‑bearers' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his'. کهتر شد/شدند is a past‑tense predication where کهتر (an adjective 'inferior/lesser') stands predicatively and شدند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'became'. The clause reads 'all his crown‑bearers became lesser/ humbled.'
segments.s733-g4: همه کهتران is 'all the lesser ones' (subject). ز (here written as زو in the line) is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating source/association, و is the conjunction 'and', توانگر is an adjective/noun 'wealthy, rich', and شدند is the plural past 'became'. The clause literally: 'all the lesser ones from him became wealthy.'
segments.s733-g5: گزین کرد is a simple past transitive 'he chose' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made -> past). از ایران is a source phrase 'from Iran'. چل و هشت هزار is a compound numeral 'forty and eight thousand' meaning 48,000; Persian sometimes expresses numerals with 'و' between tens and units and then هزار 'thousand' after the whole numeral.
segments.s733-g6: جهاندیده گردان و جنگی سوار is a noun phrase in apposition to the numeral: جهاندیده 'world‑wise/seasoned' modifies گردان 'troop(s)', and جنگی 'warlike' modifies سوار 'riders/horsemen'. The two coordinated noun phrases describe the kinds of men chosen: 'seasoned troops and battle horsemen.'
segments.s733-g7: در introduces the locative 'in/within'. گنج‌های کهن is a genitive/plural noun phrase 'the old/ancient treasuries' (گنج‌های = treasure‑houses/vaults, کهن = ancient). برگشاد is a past participle/adjectival form 'opened' placed after the noun to describe their state: 'the ancient treasuries that were opened' (or 'he opened the ancient treasuries').
segments.s733-g8: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing a relative clause. بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/establish/found' ('had founded/laid up'). پیروز و فرخ قباد are proper names; syntactically they are the agents of بنهاد ('which Piruz and Farrokh Qobad had established').
segments.s733-g81: This whole phrase names the portion and time. وزین is a poetic contraction (و + زین) meaning “and/from this” or “of this”; بهره is the noun “share/portion” that this line will specify; نیمی شب is a genitive-like phrase ‘half (نیمی) of the night (شب)’ with the quantifier before the noun; دیر یاز is a compact poetic phrase meaning ‘late (دیر) into the night’ (یاز here is an old/poetic element that helps express lateness or opening of the night).
segments.s733-g82: نشستی here functions as the past habitual ‘you used to sit’ (the expected lemma would be نشستن; the given lemma list is inconsistent). همی is the classical progressive/habitual marker giving ‘habitually/continually’. با is the preposition ‘with’. بتان is the plural of بت ‘idol’; طراز in this context most naturally reads as ‘maker/worker (one who fashions)’ or as ‘in the fashion of’, so بتان طراز means roughly ‘with the idol-makers’ (or ‘in the style/of the idols’), completing the scene of nightly company.
segments.s733-g83: همان نیز (‘that same, too’) picks up a previously mentioned person/thing and adds another grant. یک ماه is ‘one month’ (a time- or pay-unit). بر here is a preposition ‘for/on’ and چار is the numeral ‘four’ — together بر چار بهر means roughly ‘for the fourth share/part’ (classical Persian often strings prepositions and numerals like this to indicate which portion is meant).
segments.s733-g84: ببخشید is the past narrative ‘he bestowed/gave’ (from بخشیدن). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning ‘so that’; شاد باشد is the subjunctive/optative ‘may be happy’ (باد/باشد used to express wished-for or potential state). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’ and دهر means ‘the world/time’ (i.e. worldly life or fate).
segments.s733-g85: یکی بهره opens a new item: ‘one (share)’. میدان is the place/arena and is modified by the following words: چوگان و تیر list the activities/equipment ‘polo (چوگان) and arrows/archery (تیر)’, so this segment names the first share as the sporting/arena privileges.
segments.s733-g86: Another item begun again with یکی ‘one/another’. نامور is an adjective ‘renowned/famous’ describing the person; پیش او is a locative/expre ssion ‘in his presence/before him’; یادگیر is a noun ‘learner/pupil’. Word order is classical: determiner + adjective + place + possessor + noun yields ‘a famous pupil who learned in his presence.’
segments.s733-g87: دگر بهره = ‘another share’ (دگر = another). زو is the contracted poetic form of ز او ‘from him/of him’ and links the source of the share. کوه و دشت شکار lists the terrain and purpose: ‘mountain and plain (for) hunting’ — شکار is a noun modifying the places (i.e. hunting-grounds).
segments.s733-g88: This line is inverted poetically. ازان ‘from that’ introduces the result; تازه گشتی is from گشتن + تازه meaning ‘(he) became/newly turned (fresh/renewed)’ — گشتی here is a past/resultive ‘became’ rather than literal ‘went about’. ورا is the clitic ‘to him’ and روزگار (‘time/world’) is the agent or topical subject placed at the end for poetic effect: together roughly ‘from that the world made him new/renewed him.’
segments.s733-g89: هر آنگه که is a temporal subordinating phrase = "whenever/each time that" (هر = every/each + آنگه = then/at that time + که = introducing the clause). گشتی is a past/perfective verbal form of گشتن used here in the sense of having roamed/returned from an outing; ز + نخچیر marks origin/what was left the speaker (ز = from, نخچیر = prey/game). باز here is an adverb meaning "back/again" linked to the motion/return expressed by گشتی.
segments.s733-g90: به introduces the direction/purpose "to/into." رخشنده modifies روز ("bright/radiant day"). و connects روز and شب. دیر is an adjective meaning "long/lasting" or "late" modifying شب. یاز is an imperative/hortative classical form meaning "open" (یازیدن/باز کردن); syntactically it issues the action that follows the temporal clause in the previous segment.
segments.s733-g91: هر آنکس که ... is a free relative: "whoever/whichever person that ..." (هر = each/whichever, آنکس = that person, که introduces the relative clause). بودی is past of بودن (here the past copula 'was'). The conjunction و connects into the next clause; را is the definite/object marker that anticipates the object treated in the following verb phrase; پیش گاه literally "in the presence/at the audience/court" (پیش = before/in presence of, گاه = occasion/place => "the presence/audience").
segments.s733-g92: ببستی is a past-tense verb (you/one "closed/up/fastened/put under restraint"); به شهر indicates location or confinement inside the city; اندر is a classical preposition meaning "in/within." آیین و راه lists the manner or formal context ("ceremony/rite and way/manner"), i.e. he was confined in the city according to protocol/custom. The object marked by را in the previous segment is taken by ببستی here.
segments.s733-g93: دگر بهره shifts topic: دگر/دگره = "another/other," and بهره here means a share/turn/portion ("pastime/occupation/lot"); شطرنج and نرد are enumerated as that pastime (chess and backgammon). بودی is the past copula "was," so the clause states what the person's pastime/occupation was.
segments.s733-g94: سخن گفت is a light verbal phrase meaning "spoke/held conversation" (سخن = speech/topic, گفت here a noun-form glossing the act of speaking or the conversation); از introduces the object/topic of speech ("about/concerning"); روزگار نبرد = "the times/fate of battle" or more smoothly "the days/episodes of war." Grammatically this clause states the subject of conversation.
segments.s733-g95: This clause opens a new list (سه دیگر = "three others" implied by the line); هر آنکس که داننده بود is again a free relative "whoever was knowledgeable/learned." داننده is an agentive noun "one who knows/knowledgeable person" and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause selects a category of people among those three.
segments.s733-g96: فزاینده چیز is a nominal phrase "one who increases/augments things/matters" (فزاینده = augmenting/increasing, چیز = thing/matter). و خواننده بود continues with a coordinate predicate: خواننده is an agentive "reciter/reader" (in the poetic context often a reciter of tales) and بود is the copula 'was.' Together the segment describes what such a learned person did/was.
segments.s733-g73: نبودی is the past form of 'بودن' (here an emphatic/past copula, literally 'you/it was'); the following نه negates the next verbal phrase. اندیشه کردی is a past-tense, second-person construction ('you thought' or 'you did think'); ز introduces the content/source of the thought ('of/from'), and بد names that content ('evil/ill'). Together the phrase says you were not — you did not think evil.
segments.s733-g74: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus'; کز (classical contraction of که + از) introduces a relative/causal link ('that/from which' / 'such that'); ره is the noun 'way/path' and نامداران is a genitival phrase 'of the renowned' (the famed). سزد is an impersonal present/politeness verb 'it befits / it is fitting'. The whole clause means 'so that it befits the way of the renowned.'
segments.s733-g75: بهره گاه combines بهره 'a share/portion' with گاه 'time/occasion' (here 'a portion of silver' or 'a silver share, at times'). نیایش is the noun 'prayer'. بدی in this line functions as a past second‑person verb 'you did / you offered' (classical poetic usage: 'you made/offered'), so its verbal sense is not present in the provided noun senses.
segments.s733-g76: جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'World‑Creator' (the Creator). را is the Persian direct‑object marker. ستایش is the noun 'praise' (here used with an implicit verb). بدی again functions as the second‑person past verb 'you did / you offered (praise)', a verbal use not listed among the provided noun senses.
segments.s733-g77: چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth.' شمار here is a nominalized notion of counting/tally ('counting' or 'reckoning'), and سپهر بلند is a noun phrase 'the lofty/high heaven' (سپهر = sky/heavens, بلند = high/lofty). The phrase reads as 'you reckoned the fourth lofty heaven.'
segments.s733-g78: همی is a classical continuous/emphatic marker meaning 'kept on / repeatedly'; بر گرفتی is the past, second‑person 'you took up / you lifted up' (here idiomatically 'you raised' or 'you posed'). The series چه و چون و چند are interrogative words listed in a rhetorical series: چه 'what', چون 'how', چند 'how many'. Together the clause says you repeatedly raised the questions 'what, how, and how many.'
segments.s733-g79: ستاره شمر is a noun phrase 'the star Shamr' (either a star named Shamr or 'the star of Shamr'); شمر is a proper name here. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence.' بر بپای is a verbal phrase: بر (a preverb/particle) + بپای (present/habitual form of پاییدن 'to lie in wait, to wait'); together 'stood in wait before him.'
segments.s733-g80: که introduces a relative clause 'who/for he (was)'; بودی is the past copula 'he was.' به دانش is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to knowledge' (به = with/to, دانش = knowledge). ورا is the dative 'to him/his' and رهنمای is a noun 'guide/adviser.' The relative clause therefore reads 'who was to him a guide in knowledge.'
segments.s733-g105: همه روز is a temporal quantifier meaning 'every day' (همه + روز). منشور هر کشوری is a noun phrase: منشور 'charter' followed by the genitive/attributive phrase هر کشوری 'of each/ every realm' (کشوری = 'of the realm/country'). Word order is noun + postposed modifier, typical in Persian.
segments.s733-g106: نوشتی and سپردی are two past-tense verbs (2nd person singular implicit subject 'you') placed in sequence (parataxis) to mean 'you wrote and handed/entrusted'. بهر introduces a beneficiary/recipient 'for/to', and مهتری is the recipient 'a chief/elder' (postpositional phrase: بهر + noun).
segments.s733-g107: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بودی is past tense (2nd person singular) 'you were'. سر سال نو فرودین is a time expression: سر سال 'the head/start of the year', نو 'new', and فرودین the month-name (Farvardin) in apposition — together 'the start of the new year, Farvardin.'
segments.s733-g108: که introduces the following clause ('and/so/that'), رخشان شدی is predicate: رخشان 'radiant' + شدی (past 2nd sing.) 'you became'. در دل = 'in (your) heart' (در + noun). از marks source or cause 'from/of', and هور دین is a compound meaning 'the splendor/light of religion' (هور 'splendor/light', دین 'religion').
segments.s733-g109: نهادی is a past action (2nd person singular) from نهادن 'to place/put/hide' — 'you placed/you hid'. یکی گنج = 'one treasure' (یکی as an indefinite/ numeral before the noun). خسرو is the possessor 'of Khosrow/the king' (postposed), and نهان is an adjective 'hidden' placed after the noun as is common in Persian.
segments.s733-g110: که introduces the result/relative clause that follows. نشناختی is the negative past form (ن- + شناختی) meaning 'you did not let know / you did not recognize' — here functionally 'did not let [others] know' or 'it was not known'. کهتری functions as 'a lesser (one)/an inferior' (used substantively). در جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase closing the clause: 'so that no lesser person in the world knew it.'
segments.s733-g25: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well/safely' (به used adverbially + خوبی). مر is an archaic clitic meaning 'me' (dative/benefactive) placed before the object; او را is the definite object 'him' (او with object marker را). به راه آورید is an imperative (2nd person plural) of آوردن 'bring/lead onto the road': آورید is the command, and به راه is the directional complement 'onto the road.'
segments.s733-g26: کزین = کز + این 'from/away from this' (poetic preposition + demonstrative). بگذرد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive ('he may pass' / 'he get past') used to express result or purpose. بند و چاه are the objects of آورید in the previous imperative and mean 'fetters (bonds) and a pit/trap'; آورید here again is imperative 'bring' (give/bring these things).
segments.s733-g27: به هرسو is a prepositional/directional phrase 'to every direction/everywhere' (به = toward; هرسو = every direction). فرستید is an imperative (2nd person plural) of فرستادن 'send/dispatch.' کارآگهان is an agent noun 'the informed/knowledgeable ones' (plural) — the people to be sent; it functions as the direct object of فرستید.
segments.s733-g28: بدان is formed from the prefix بـ (here attached to دان) + دان 'know' and functions imperatively/causatively here — 'inform them' / 'let them know.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' and governs نماند, which is a negative jussive/optative 'may not remain' (نَ + ماند). سخن در نهان = 'any talk/speech in secret' (سخن 'speech/word', در نهان 'in secret').
segments.s733-g29: طلایه 'the vanguard' is the subject; بباید is a modal/impersonal form of باید meaning 'must/ought to' (here giving an order). به‌روز و شبان are temporal adverbials 'by day and by night' (به‌روز = 'by day / of the day'; شبان is an archaic adverbial 'by night'). Together they specify the required time of the vanguard's duty.
segments.s733-g30: مخسپید (literary/archaic compound meaning 'the white‑headed' or 'elders') functions as a noun phrase referring to the aged; the verb is omitted (common in classical Persian) or understood from بایست/باید — here it carries the obligation implied earlier. در خیمه بی‌پاسبان is a prepositional phrase 'in a tent unguarded' (در = in; خیمه = tent; بی‌پاسبان = without guards/unguarded) that describes where they should sleep.
segments.s733-g31: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' لشکر is the genitive/possessive 'army/host.' ده و دو هزار دگر is a numeric expression 'ten and two thousand (i.e. twelve thousand) more/others' — دگر here means 'additional/other' and attaches to the numeric phrase.
segments.s733-g32: دلاور is an adjective/noun 'valiant/brave,' سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (the head noun), and پرخاشخر is a descriptive adjective 'aggressive/quarrelsome' modifying سواران; the whole is a noun phrase apposing the preceding number to describe those troops.
segments.s733-g33: بخواند is a 3rd-person past narrative form with the old preverb b- (here rendered as 'he called/uttered'); it coordinates with the following clause via و. بسی is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'much' or 'many' modifying هدیه‌ها ('gifts'). دادشان is the past verb داد ('gave') with the enclitic object شان 'them' (i.e. 'he gave them'). Together the clause means 'he called/summoned and gave them many gifts.'
segments.s733-g34: به راه الانان is a prepositional phrase of direction: به + راه 'toward/on the road' + الانان 'the Alans' (the destination). فرستادشان is the past tense verb فرستادن 'to send' with the 3rd-person singular subject and the enclitic شان 'them' as object; it takes the preceding phrase as its goal. The clause means 'he sent them off toward the Alans.'
segments.s733-g35: بدیشان is an old-form dative 'to them' (equivalent to به ایشان). سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over.' آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying در, and در here means 'door' or 'gate' (not the locative preposition). باختر is a locational adjective 'western' or 'in the west.' The whole clause: 'he entrusted to them that western gate.'
segments.s733-g36: بدان here functions as 'to that / to it' (referring back to the gate) formed with the prepositional/preverbal b-; تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that.' نیاید is a negative optative/subjunctive meaning 'may not come' or 'should not come.' ز is poetic 'from,' دشمن is 'enemy,' and گذر is 'passage/passing' (i.e. an enemy incursion). The clause means 'so that no passage may come from the enemy.'
segments.s733-g37: بدان is again a dative-like 'to those/to that' (formed with b-); سرکشان addresses the addressees (here 'the watchmen/overseers' — an epic term for those on guard). گفت is the past verb 'he said.' بیدار is an admonition/adjective 'awake/alert' and the following بید is a fixed poetic vocative/imperative particle meaning roughly 'be' or an emphatic 'be awake.' The clause reports his spoken command: 'He said to the watchmen, "Be alert!"'.
segments.s733-g38: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. در پناه is a prepositional phrase 'in the protection of'; پناه here means 'protection/refuge.' جهاندار is a title 'the world-ruler' or sovereign. The final بید functions as a poetic copula/imperative-like form (here best read as 'be'/'are' in the sense 'all are in the sovereign's protection'). The line gives both the command and the reassurance: 'All are in the protection of the sovereign.'
segments.s733-g39: ده ودو هزار is a classical way to state the numeral 'ten and two thousand' = 'twelve thousand' (composition of numerals in epic style). دگر 'another/additional' modifies the numeral, and برگزید is the past of برگزیدن 'to choose/select.' The clause means 'he selected another twelve thousand.'
segments.s733-g40: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from.' مردان جنگی is a genitival noun phrase 'men of war' = 'warlike men/warriors.' چنان چون is a correlative pair meaning 'such as / in the manner that' and sزید (سزید) is the past of سزا/سزایدن used here to mean 'befit / be fitting.' Together the phrase means 'from the warlike men, in the way that was fitting (i.e. the proper or deserving ones).'
segments.s733-g65: This is a nominal prepositional phrase meaning 'concerning the affairs of the army and of the world'. ز is the poetic preposition marking 'about/from' (here: 'concerning'); کار means 'affair/affairs' and sits before its noun (سپاه, جهان) in the characteristic Persian noun compound order 'affair of X'. The two halves are joined by و.
segments.s733-g66: بگفتی is the past-tense verbal form (prefix بـ + past stem + ­ی ending). In Ferdowsi's narrative usage this form conveys a past report ('he told / spoke'), even though morphologically it looks like 2nd-person; it functions here as the main past verb. به شاه is the indirect object 'to the king'. آشکار و نهان are adverbial/adjectival opposites 'openly and secretly' placed after the verb to describe the manner of speaking.
segments.s733-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. در پادشاهی ('in the kingdom/kingship') is the locative phrase placed before the verb. به دیدی is the past-seeing verb form (بـ + دید + ی); here it functions as the reporting past 'you/he saw'. شکست is the direct object 'defeat'. Word order is: temporal/subordinate marker + locative phrase + verb + object.
segments.s733-g68: This phrase names the possible source/agent of the defeat. ز before لشکر‌گر marks 'by/through' or 'from' (cause/agent). لشکر‌گر is an agent noun 'army-raiser/commander'. از مردم 'from among the people' uses از to mark 'from/out of', and زیر دست is a compound meaning 'those under (one's) hand/power' (زیر = under, دست = power/control). The whole string gives alternatives for the cause of the defeat.
segments.s733-g69: سبک دامن داد is an idiomatic phrase: سبک 'light' + دامن 'skirt' + داد 'gave' literally 'gave a light skirt', i.e. 'he loosened/raised his skirt' — a poetic way to say 'he quickened/run off'. بر introduces the target; تافتی is a participial/relative form from تافتن (here 'to turn/leave/flee') meaning 'the one who had turned/fled'. The clause therefore describes a quick movement toward (or upon) the fleeing one.
segments.s733-g70: گذشته is a past participle/adverbial 'having gone / gone past'. بجستی is the past form of بجستن (here 'to spring/leap') — used as a past action 'he leapt/sprang'. و joins the final verb دریافتی, which is the past 'he received/obtained' used here in the sense 'he seized/captured'. The sequence describes pursuit (leaping upon what had gone) and capture.
segments.s733-g71: دگر means 'again' or 'another (time)'. بهر is the preposition 'for' (بهر شادی و رامشگران = 'for joy and for peacemakers'). رامشگران is a plural agent noun 'peacemakers/comforters'; coordination with شادی links the purpose of the action.
segments.s733-g72: نشسته is a predicative participle/adjective 'seated' (state of sitting). به آرام is an adverbial phrase 'in calm/quietly', and با مهتران 'with the chiefs' indicates accompaniment. Note the typical Persian order: stative verb/adjective followed by adverbial and accompanying phrase.
segments.s733-g9: جهان را is the definite object (جهان + را). ببخشید is an archaic past-tense form of بخشیدن here meaning 'gave/assigned' (i.e. 'apportioned'); بر چار بهر is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon four sides' = 'into four parts' (بر = on/toward, چار = 'four', بهر = an older word for 'side/direction'). The phrase as a whole means 'he allotted the world into four quarters.'
segments.s733-g10: یکایک همه = 'each and every / all of them' (emphatic distributive). نامزد here functions as an appointment/nomination (older usage: نامزد کردن = 'to appoint/name'); کرد is the past of کردن 'made/did/appointed'; شهر follows as the noun governed by that action (typical SOV order in older Persian). The whole clause: 'one by one he appointed them all to the city.'
segments.s733-g11: از آن نامداران = 'from those renowned ones' (از = from; آن = those/the; نامداران = renowned men). ده و دو هزار is a classical numeric expression meaning 'twelve thousand' (old compound number ordering).
segments.s733-g12: گزین کرد = 'he chose/selected' (گزین as verb 'to choose' + کرد past auxiliary). ز ایران و نیران سوار = 'riders/cavalry from Iran and Nīrān' (ز = from (poetic); ایران = Iran; نیران here names the other group/land; سوار = 'riders/horsemen').
segments.s733-g13: فرستاد خسرو = 'Khosrow sent' (فرستاد past 'sent', followed by the subject name خسرو). سوی مرز روم = 'toward the Roman border' (سوی = toward; مرز روم = the border/frontier of Rome/Byzantium). Word order is verb + subject + adverbial destination.
segments.s733-g14: نگهبان آن فرخ آزاد بوم = 'the guardian of that fortunate, free land' (نگهبان = guard/guardian; آن = that/the; فرخ = 'fortunate/happy' used adjectivally; آزاد = 'free' or 'noble' here as an adjective; بوم = homeland/native soil). Adjectives follow here the noun they modify in classical word-order.
segments.s733-g15: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from ب- + دان (b- prefix + دان 'to know'); تا introduces the purpose/limit 'so that' or 'lest' (introducing the intended/preventive clause). ز روم اندر ایران سپاه = 'an army from Rome into Iran' (ز = from; روم = Rome/Byzantium; اندر ایران = 'into/in Iran'; سپاه = army). The clause sets the purpose/concern: 'know (this) so that an army from Rome does not enter Iran.'
segments.s733-g16: نیاید is a negative-jussive/present 'may not come' (expressing prohibition/hope). که is the complementizer 'that'. کشور شود = 'the realm becomes' (شود = become, here subjunctive). زو تباه = 'from it / by it ruined' (زو is an older contraction meaning 'از + او' = 'by/through it'); تباه = 'ruined/destroyed'. The whole clause: 'may it not come, that the realm become ruined by it.'
segments.s733-g41: به سوی خراسان is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward Khorasan'); the verb فرستادشان is past tense 'sent' with the object clitic -شان 'them' attached. The subject is omitted (third person singular) as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s733-g42: بسی is a quantifier here ('much/many') modifying the nouns پند و اندرزها (a compound plural 'advice and counsels'); دادشان is a past-tense verb 'gave' with the same clitic -شان 'them'. Word order places the quantifier + nouns before the verb, and the clitic marks the direct object.
segments.s733-g43: که introduces this subordinate clause ('that'); از ... تا marks a range 'from X to Y' (از مرز هیتال تا مرزچین = 'from the border of Hital to the border/limit called Marzchin'). This is a simple prepositional range construction.
segments.s733-g44: نباید is an impersonal modal 'should/must not'; it governs the clause that follows. کس is the indefinite 'anyone'; پی نهد is a verbal phrase ('پی' + root نَهَد here = 'to set/put/step after' → 'to set foot/step'), and بر زمین is the locative 'on the ground/land'. The negation is expressed by placing نباید before the infinitival/clausal verb.
segments.s733-g45: مگر introduces an exception 'except/unless'; به آگهی و بفرمان ما are two coordinating prepositional phrases meaning 'with our notice' and 'by our order' respectively. بفرمان is a frozen compound meaning 'by order (of)', taking the genitive pronoun ما.
segments.s733-g46: روان بسته دارد is a verbal phrase in which بسته (bound/tied) describes the state and دارد is the present-tense main verb 'has/keeps'. Here روان functions idiomatically (an older usage) referring to the moving force/people or their state; together the phrase means 'keeps (them) bound/restrained'. به پیمان ما ('to/under our covenant') marks the relation or condition they are kept under.
segments.s733-g47: بهر کشوری is a distributive prepositional phrase 'for each country/realm'; گنج آگنده هست is an existential/assertive clause meaning 'there is a full/filled treasure' (آگنده = 'filled/abundant'), with هست as the copula/existential verb. Word order places the prepositional phrase fronted for emphasis.
segments.s733-g48: که introduces a relative/result clause. کس را uses را as a marker to mark the affected person ('anyone'); نباید شدن is the impersonal prohibition construction ('should not become / should not be made'), where شدن is the infinitival 'to become' and نباید negates/forbids it. دوردست is the predicate adjective 'far-off/distant' describing the resulting state.
segments.s733-g97: به نوبت is an adverbial phrase meaning “in turn” (به + noun نوبت). و is the coordinating particle “and/then” linking the actions. را is the Persian definite-object marker attached to an omitted object (those people/reciters). پیش here means “in the presence of / before” and بنشاندی is an archaic past 2nd-person singular form (بنَشاندی) meaning “you seated/placed (caused to sit).” The whole chunk = “you seated (them) in turn and set (them) before (someone).”
segments.s733-g98: سخنهای is the plural construct ‘words/sayings/tales’ (سخن + plural/izāfa); دیرینه is an adjective “ancient/old.” برخواندی is a classical perfect/imperfect form with the prefix بر- plus خواندی (past stem of خواندن) meaning “you/one would recite/read aloud” (here used as a past habitual/performative). Together: “(you) recited the ancient sayings.”
segments.s733-g99: چهارم is a sequential/discourse numeral “fourth” marking the next item. فرستادگان is the plural ‘envoys/messengers’ and را is the object marker attaching to them. ز راه is a prepositional phrase “from/by the road” (ز = poetic “from/of,” راه = road/way), indicating their origin or approach.
segments.s733-g100: همی‌خواندندی is a classical/perfect-imperfect verbal form with the prefix همی- indicating habitual/ongoing action; here it means roughly “(you) would summon/call/they would read” depending on context — in this line it functions as a habitual past verb ‘would call/bring’ or ‘would have read/recited.’ به نزدیک شاه is a goal phrase “to near the king / into the king’s presence” (به = to, نزدیک = near, شاه = king).
segments.s733-g101: نوشتی is the past 2nd-person singular of نوشتن “you wrote.” همه modifies پاسخ‌نامه and means “all/the whole.” پاسخ‌نامه is the compound noun “reply-letter/response.” باز is the adverb “again” placed after the object. Word order here is verb – (modifier) – object – adverb, typical of classical poetic diction.
segments.s733-g102: بدادی is intended as the past 2nd-person singular of دادن “you gave/returned,” but the supplied lemma list does not include that sense (hence -1 below). بدَان is the preposition به + demonstrative آن contracted (literary spelling split here) meaning “to that.” مرد گردن‌فراز is the noun plus epithet “the proud man” (مرد = man, گردن‌فراز = prideful/proud), the indirect object introduced by به/بدان.
segments.s733-g103: فرستاده is the subject “the envoy/messenger.” با introduces the accompanying things “with”; خلعت is “robe of honour/the bestowed robe,” و is “and,” کام خویش = “his desire/wish/satisfaction” with خویش (here written خوی + enclitic ش) marking possession “his.” The phrase means the envoy returned bearing the gift and with his desire fulfilled.
segments.s733-g104: ز در is the locative/origin phrase “from the gate/door” (ز = from, در = door/gate). بازگشتی is a classical past-form/participle meaning “returned” (here describing the envoy’s action). به آرام خویش is the destination/complement “to his own calm/peace” (به = to, آرام = calm/peace, خوی + ش = his own). The sequence gives the motion: ‘returned from the gate to his own peace.’
segments.s733-g49: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'/'if'). باید is the modal 'ought/must'. بخواهید is the present/subjunctive (2nd-person plural/formal) of خواستن 'to desire/wish' and follows باید to mean 'ought to want/seek'. و simply coordinates. خرم is an adjectival predicate 'glad/joyful'; بوید here is an archaic poetic copula (from بودن) meaning 'be' rather than its literal modern senses, so the phrase means 'and be glad.'
segments.s733-g50: خردمند باشید is an exhortation using باشید (2nd-person plural/formal subjunctive/imperative) meaning 'be wise.' و links the two predicates. بی‌غم is an adjectival phrase 'without grief/carefree'; بوید again is the poetic copula 'be,' yielding 'be carefree.' The sequence gives two coordinated imperatives/admonitions.
segments.s733-g51: در گنج places the location first ('in the treasure/vault'); بگشاد is a verbal form meaning 'he opened' (the prefix بـ with past stem gives a past action). و coordinates to the next phrase; چندی درم is a noun phrase 'some dirhams' (چندی = 'some/several', درم = coin/small sum) serving as the object taken from the treasure.
segments.s733-g52: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'چندی درم' ('dirhams which…'). بودی is the past-tense copula 'were/was' (here plural/remote past for the coins). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; هرمز names the authority/place of issue (here a personal/royal name). برو here is used in the narrative sense 'placed/put' or 'went to' (contextually 'put/laid'); بر رقم is a prepositional phrase 'on the tally/record' (بر = on/upon, رقم = number/record). Together the clause specifies the provenance of the coins and what was done with them.
segments.s733-g53: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought' (narrative perfect). و = 'and' links actions. گریان is a participial/adverbial adjective 'weeping' modifying the verb phrase. به marks the indirect object 'to/for'; درویش is the indirect-object noun 'the dervish' (here a poor/ascetic recipient). داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' The order is typical subject (implied) + verb + manner phrase + indirect object + verb 'gave.'
segments.s733-g54: چو begins a subordinate clause ('when/as'). درویش is the clause subject; پیوسته is an adverb 'continually/constantly' modifying the verb. بد is functioning adverbially here ('ill/poorly' or 'in a weak/inadequate way') modifying داد; بیش داد literally 'gave more' (بیش = more, داد = gave). The clause thus reports that when the dervish was continually (in that state/coming repeatedly), he (the giver) gave yet more — the combination of بد with بیش داد is idiomatic/poetic and describes repeated or excessive giving in an adverse context.
segments.s733-g55: از آنکس که introduces a relative phrase 'from that person who…' (از + آنکس = 'from that one'). که introduces the relative clause. او is the pronoun 'he' (referring back to the mentioned person). یار is 'companion/ally' and بندوی is an attributive noun meaning 'bondman/servant/devotee' — together 'his companion and bondman/devotee.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole segment identifies provenance/association with a named follower.
segments.s733-g56: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near/close to' (به = to/at, نزدیک = near). گستهم here is a proper name (a hero, spelled Gostaham in Shahnameh) and thus functions as the first object of the preposition; و coordinates the second name. زنگوی (Zanguy/Zanguey) is the second personal name. بود is the past copula 'was' attaching to the whole prepositional phrase: 'he was near Gostaham and Zanguy.'
segments.s733-g57: 'که' begins a relative clause ('who/which'); 'بودند' is the past plural copula ('they were'); 'یازان' is the predicate noun after the copula (an archaic/proper name or group-label here — the exact literary referent is specialized/poetic); 'به خون پدر' uses 'به' with an accusative-cum-causal sense ('with/for/by the blood of the father'), indicating the motive/cause tying these people to the father's blood. Word order is relative-clause style: relative pronoun + copula + predicate + prepositional cause.
segments.s733-g58: This is a compact past-tense result clause: 'ز' (poetic 'from') introduces the source ('from their bodies'); 'تنهای' (plural/construct of 'body') + 'ایشان' ('their/them') form the genitival/possessive phrase 'their bodies'; 'جدا کرد' is the compound verb 'separated/caused apart' (past tense, third-person singular); the final noun 'سر' is the thing separated (head) and therefore the direct object placed after the verb for emphasis. The sequence literally reads 'from their bodies apart made head' → 'he severed the head from their bodies.'
segments.s733-g59: 'چو' here = 'when'; 'از کین و نفرین' = 'out of/from hatred and curse' (an ablative/causal phrase explaining motive); 'به پردخت شاه' is a difficult poetic phrasing meaning 'to the king's attention/consideration' (literally 'to the paying/handling of the king' — 'به' marks the direction/target and 'پردخت' is the noun/abstract verbal form). The whole segment sets the temporal-causal frame: 'When, from hatred and curses, [it] came to the king.'
segments.s733-g60: 'بدانش' is a compact deictic/dir. phrase ('to that/to him' in older usage) linking back to what reached the king; 'یکی' here means 'someone' (an indefinite person); 'دیگر' = 'other/another'; 'آورد' is past 'brought' (here 'proposed' or 'presented'); 'راه' = 'way/course/plan'. Together: 'another person brought/put forward another way to him.'
segments.s733-g61: 'از آن پس' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' or 'from then on'; 'شب و روز' is the fixed pair 'night and day'; 'گردنده دهر' is a poetic genitive/epithet: 'the revolving/turning time' or 'the turning world/age' — 'گردنده' (rotating) modifies 'دهر' (time/age). The whole phrase names the onward passage of time: 'from then on (the) turning nights and days of time...'.
segments.s733-g62: 'نشست' is used as a past action ('he sat' or 'a session was held'); it coordinates with the next verb via 'و'. 'ببخشید' here takes the sense 'bestowed/gave out' (not 'forgave'); 'بر چار' = 'to/forth to the four' (i.e., distributed among four) and 'بهر' = 'share/for' — the phrase means 'he apportioned out (gave) for the four shares.' Grammatically the two verbs are conjoined and the prepositional phrase 'بر چار بهر' shows the recipients/recipients' quota.
segments.s733-g63: 'از آن چار' = 'from those four'; 'یک بهر' = 'one share' (literally 'one for/portion'); 'موبد' is the dative/beneficiary noun 'to the mobad' (Zoroastrian priest); 'نهاد' is the past tense of 'نهادن' ('placed/established/put') — here it means 'he assigned/put (a share) for the mobad.' Word order is 'from those four one share mobad placed' → 'he assigned one of the four to the mobad.'
segments.s733-g64: 'که' introduces a restrictive relative clause ('who/that'); 'دارد' is present tense 'has/possesses' used here as a verb of possession/capacity; 'سخنهای' is the plural construct 'words/sayings'; 'نیکو' = 'good/fine'; 'بیاد' here functions adverbially/locatively 'in memory' or 'in mind' — so the clause reads 'who has good sayings in memory' (i.e., 'who remembers good words' / 'who is mindful of good speech').
segments.s733-g17: مگر here functions as an exception/conditional 'if/except'; هرکسی is an indefinite subject 'whoever/anyone'. برکند is a classical/poetic present-subjunctive/optative (3rd sg) of برکندن (بر‑ + کندن) meaning 'uproot/abandon/tear away' and sits before its object; مرز خویش = 'his own border' (مرز = border; خویش = 'one's own', formed here as خوی + ش).
segments.s733-g18: بداند is 3rd-singular present/subjunctive of دانستن used optatively/jussively here 'let him know / he should know'. سر مایه is a compound in which سر functions as 'chief/top' modifying مایه 'resource, capital'; و is the coordinating 'and'; ارز خویش = 'his wealth/value' where خویش (خوی + ش) is the possessive 'his/one's own'. Word order is Verb + (object phrase) in this poetic register.
segments.s733-g19: هم از = 'also/from' (هم = 'also/together', از = 'from/of'). نامداران is the plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. ده و دو هزار is a poetic numeric expression 'ten and two thousand' = 'twelve thousand' (note the conjunction و between the numerals). The whole segment supplies the source/number of the troops.
segments.s733-g20: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (head noun). هشیار and خنجرگزار are adjectival modifiers following the noun: هشیار = 'alert/alert-minded', خنجرگزار = 'dagger-bearing' (literally 'one who carries a dagger'); in classical Persian adjectives commonly follow the noun they modify.
segments.s733-g21: بدان here is a compact/poetic 'thereupon/with that' (written as ب + دان in the tokenization); تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'. سوی = 'toward', زابلستان is a place-name (Zabulistan/Sistan). شوند is 3rd‑pl. subjunctive/present of شدن 'become/go' and expresses the intended/resulting motion ('may they go/become').
segments.s733-g22: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; بوم 'homeland/native soil' modified by سیه 'black/dark' (here a descriptive epithet). در گلستان شوند = 'become (or be placed) in the Golestān/rose‑garden'; گلستان is literal 'rose-garden' and شوند repeats the 3rd‑pl. subjunctive. The clause describes a transformation or movement from a 'dark land' into a pleasant garden — typical poetic contrast.
segments.s733-g23: بدیشان is the fused prepositional phrase (به + ایشان) 'to them'; چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت is the past 3rd‑singular of گفتن 'he said' (here reported speech marker). هرکو = 'whoever/anyone'; ز = poetic 'from'; راه = 'road/way'. Together they introduce direct speech and the conditional protasis 'whoever from the road...'.
segments.s733-g24: بگردد is 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive of گشتن/گردیدن 'turn/stray/go astray' (here 'turn aside/from the road'). ندارد is present 'does not have'; زبان را is the definite object 'the tongue' used metonymically for 'speech'. نگاه is a noun 'gaze/view' in isolation, but in idiomatic classical Persian this fragment evokes نگه (or نگاه داشتن) 'keeping/restraint' — so the clause idiomatically means 'will not have the keeping/restraint of the tongue' (i.e. will lack restraint in speech or will be punished/ruined). Word order places negated verb before object.

sentences.s733-s1: After that, when the king's power spread far and wide, the whole world became his well-wisher.
sentences.s733-s2: All his crown‑bearers became lesser; all the lesser men from him grew wealthy.
sentences.s733-s3: He chose from Iran forty‑eight thousand seasoned troops and war‑horse riders.
sentences.s733-s4: He opened the ancient treasuries which Piruz and Farrokh Qobad had founded.
sentences.s733-s41: From that portion you spent half the night, sitting late together with idol-makers.
sentences.s733-s42: Likewise, he granted that same one a month’s allowance for the fourth share, so that he might be happy in the world.
sentences.s733-s43: One share was the field for polo and archery; another was a renowned pupil who learned in his presence.
sentences.s733-s44: Another share was, from him, mountain and plain for the hunt; from that the world renewed him.
sentences.s733-s45: Whenever you came back from the hunt, open (it) to the radiant day and the long night.
sentences.s733-s46: Whoever he was, when you brought him before the audience you confined him in the city according to ceremony and custom.
sentences.s733-s47: Another's diversion was chess and backgammon; they spoke of the times of battle.
sentences.s733-s48: Three others: whoever was learned was one who augmented matters and was a reciter.
sentences.s733-s37: You did not think ill, in such a way that it would be fitting for the path of the renowned.
sentences.s733-s38: You gave a share of silver and at times offered prayer; you praised the World‑Creator.
sentences.s733-s39: You counted the fourth high heaven, and repeatedly raised the questions of what, how, and how many.
sentences.s733-s40: The star Shamr stood in wait before him, for he was his guide in matters of knowledge.
sentences.s733-s53: Every day you wrote a charter for every realm and entrusted it to a chief.
sentences.s733-s54: When you were at the start of the new year, Farvardin, you became radiant in your heart from the splendor of religion.
sentences.s733-s55: You hid one of Khosrow's treasures in secret so that no lesser person in the world knew it.
sentences.s733-s13: Bring him to me safely onto the road; take fetters and a pit so he cannot get away from here.
sentences.s733-s14: Send informed agents everywhere to let them know, so that no word remains hidden.
sentences.s733-s15: The vanguard must keep watch by day and by night; the white‑haired (elders) should sleep in tents without guards.
sentences.s733-s16: From the army, another twelve thousand valiant horsemen, fierce and quarrelsome.
sentences.s733-s17: He called out and gave them many gifts, and sent them on their way toward the Alans.
sentences.s733-s18: He entrusted to them that gate in the west so that no passage might come from the enemy.
sentences.s733-s19: To those watchmen he said: 'Be alert, be! All are under the protection of the sovereign.'
sentences.s733-s20: He selected another twelve thousand from the warlike men, as was fitting.
sentences.s733-s33: He/you spoke to the king, openly and in secret, about the affairs of the army and the affairs of the world.
sentences.s733-s34: When in the kingdom you saw a defeat — whether from the army-commander or from the people under (one's) power.
sentences.s733-s35: He loosened his skirt to run and sprang upon the one who had turned away; the one who had gone he leapt at and seized.
sentences.s733-s36: Again, for joy and for peacemakers, he sat quietly at ease with the chiefs.
sentences.s733-s5: He divided the world into four quarters; one by one he appointed them all to the city.
sentences.s733-s6: From those renowned men he selected twelve thousand cavalry from Iran and the Nīrān.
sentences.s733-s7: Khosrow sent (them) toward the Roman frontier, the guardian of that fortunate, free land.
sentences.s733-s8: Know this: so that an army from Rome do not come into Iran, lest the realm be ruined by it.
sentences.s733-s21: He sent them toward Khorasan and gave them abundant advice and counsels.
sentences.s733-s22: That from the border of Hital to the frontier of China no one should set foot on the land.
sentences.s733-s23: Except with our notice and by our command, he shall keep them bound to our covenant.
sentences.s733-s24: For each country there is a well-stocked treasury, so that no one should be made distant.
sentences.s733-s49: You would seat them in turn and place them before (one), and you would read aloud the ancient sayings.
sentences.s733-s50: Fourth, you would call the envoys coming by the road into the king’s presence.
sentences.s733-s51: You wrote again all the reply-letters and gave them back to that proud man.
sentences.s733-s52: The envoy, with his robe of honour and his wish fulfilled, returned from the gate to his own peace.
sentences.s733-s25: When you ought to desire and be glad, be wise and be free of sorrow.
sentences.s733-s26: He opened the treasure and (took out) some dirhams, which were from Hormoz, and put them on the tally/record.
sentences.s733-s27: He brought (it) and, weeping, gave it to the dervish; when the dervish kept on (asking/being present), he gave still more.
sentences.s733-s28: From that man who was his devotee/servant-companion, he was (close) to Gostaham and to Zanguy.
sentences.s733-s29: He severed the heads from the bodies of those Yāzān who had shed their father's blood.
sentences.s733-s30: When, because of hatred and curse, this reached the king, another man proposed a different course.
sentences.s733-s31: After that, the revolving days and nights of time continued, and he sat and apportioned out four shares.
sentences.s733-s32: From those four he set aside one share for the mobad (priest), who keeps good sayings in memory.
sentences.s733-s9: If anyone abandons (or uproots) his own border, let him know his principal capital and wealth.
sentences.s733-s10: Also, from the renowned men, twelve thousand horsemen — alert, dagger-bearing.
sentences.s733-s11: Thereupon (so that) they were sent toward Zabulistan; from the dark homeland they would become (or be placed) in the rose-garden.
sentences.s733-s12: He said to them thus: whoever turns aside from the road will not have (the keeping of) his tongue (i.e., will lack restraint of speech).
